title: Liquid Basics
summary: Learn what Liquid is, where it runs, and how output tags, logic tags, loops, and conditionals work in real storefront templates.
concepts: Liquid renders on the server before the browser runs JavaScript.
Use {{ }} to output values and {% %} for logic, assignment, loops, and control flow.
The most common starter patterns are if/unless, for loops, assign, capture, and case.
Real theme work mixes Liquid with HTML, CSS, and JavaScript instead of replacing them.

quiz.question: Where does Liquid execute?
options: On Shopify's server before HTML is sent
quiz.explanation: Liquid is a server-side templating language.

quiz.question: Which syntax outputs a value?
quiz.explanation: Double curly braces output values.

videos.title: Getting started with Shopify Liquid
videos.description: Solid beginner explanation of core Liquid ideas.

videos.title: Shopify Theme Development - Liquid Full Tutorial
videos.description: Longer coding walkthrough that shows Liquid inside a real theme.

title: Objects, Filters, Loops, and Conditionals
summary: Use product, collection, cart, customer, and shop data with filters, for loops, and conditional logic.
concepts: Objects expose store data such as product, collection, cart, customer, request, section, and settings.
Filters transform values for money, images, dates, text, arrays, and HTML safety.
Loops are useful for rendering product media, tags, recommendations, menu items, or cart lines.
Conditionals protect your theme against missing data and control storefront states.

quiz.question: What does a Liquid filter do?
quiz.explanation: Filters modify values before they are printed.

quiz.question: Which object is best for storefront cart totals?
quiz.explanation: The cart object exposes totals, lines, note, and attributes.

videos.title: Getting started with Shopify Liquid
videos.description: Concept-first walkthrough of how Liquid pieces fit together.

videos.title: Shopify Theme Development - Liquid Full Tutorial
videos.description: Practical theme coding examples for data output and loops.

title: render vs include
summary: Understand why render is the modern pattern, how scope works, and how to migrate old themes away from include.
concepts: render is the modern snippet API and isolates scope more safely than include.
include is deprecated in modern Shopify theme practice and appears mostly in older themes.
Explicitly pass variables to render to make snippet contracts clearer and easier to debug.
Migration usually means replacing implicit scope assumptions with named parameters.

quiz.question: Why is render preferred over include?
options: It provides clearer scope and is the supported modern pattern
It is required for JSON templates only
quiz.explanation: render gives safer, clearer variable scope.

quiz.question: What is the best migration habit when replacing include?
options: Pass only the data the snippet needs
quiz.explanation: Explicit parameters make snippets easier to maintain.

videos.title: Getting started with Shopify Liquid
videos.description: Useful theory base before discussing snippet scope.

videos.title: Shopify Theme Development - Liquid Full Tutorial
videos.description: Practical theme workflow where snippet rendering patterns show up.

title: Template Types and Real Shopify Data
summary: Work across product, collection, page, cart, article, blog, search, and customer templates with the right context objects.
concepts: Each template type has its own object context and expected UX responsibilities.
product templates care about media, variants, price, inventory, and merchandising blocks.
collection and search templates focus on pagination, filters, sorting, and card rendering.
cart, article, blog, and customer templates introduce very different data shapes and empty states.

quiz.question: Which object is central in a product template?
quiz.explanation: The product object drives product template rendering.

quiz.question: Why does template context matter?
options: Because every page type exposes the same data
Because page types expose different data and UX concerns
quiz.explanation: Different template types require different objects and edge-case handling.

videos.title: Shopify Theme Development - Liquid Theme Architecture
videos.description: Theme architecture context for understanding template types.

videos.title: How To Code Your Own Shopify Theme
videos.description: A real theme build where templates and store data come together.

title: Creating Sections from Scratch
summary: Build reusable theme sections from zero and learn how sections connect templates, schema, and merchant editing.
concepts: Sections are merchant-editable modules rendered by templates or JSON templates.
A section file mixes HTML, Liquid, CSS classes, and a schema block.
Good sections have strong defaults, clear setting labels, and resilient empty states.
A section should have one clear purpose so merchants understand how to configure it.
code:
{{ section.settings.heading }}

{{ section.settings.copy }}

{% schema %} { "name": "Hero Banner", "settings": [ { "type": "text", "id": "heading", "label": "Heading" }, { "type": "textarea", "id": "copy", "label": "Copy" } ], "presets": [{ "name": "Hero Banner" }] } {% endschema %}

quiz.question: What makes a section editable in the theme editor?
quiz.explanation: Schema defines merchant-editable settings and editor metadata.

quiz.question: What is a good section design habit?
quiz.explanation: Focused sections are easier to use and maintain.

videos.title: Learn Shopify Sections & Schema with Examples
videos.description: Clear introduction to sections and schema fundamentals.

videos.title: Shopify Theme Development - Sections Tutorial
videos.description: Hands-on theme section build from a developer perspective.

title: Blocks and Dynamic Layouts
summary: Use blocks to let merchants reorder, duplicate, and control content layouts inside sections.
concepts: Blocks let merchants add repeated content units inside a section.
Block types are ideal for headings, icons, CTAs, media cards, testimonials, or FAQ rows.
Your Liquid should switch block rendering by block.type and use block.shopify_attributes in editor-aware markup.
Good blocks balance flexibility with constraints so designs do not break.

quiz.question: Why are blocks useful?
options: They allow merchant-controlled repeated layouts inside a section
quiz.explanation: Blocks make sections modular and reorderable.

quiz.question: What helps theme editor usability inside loops?
quiz.explanation: block.shopify_attributes improves editor behavior and targeting.

videos.title: Learn Shopify Sections & Schema with Examples
videos.description: Explains the theory behind configurable section layouts.

videos.title: How to Build Shopify Theme Sections from Scratch
videos.description: A practical block-based section implementation walkthrough.

title: All Section Setting Types
summary: Work with text, image, richtext, url, range, checkbox, select, radio, color, product, collection, and list settings.
concepts: The right setting type reduces merchant confusion and avoids brittle content entry.
Use text and textarea for simple copy, richtext for formatted content, and url for link targets.
Range, select, checkbox, radio, and color give safe UI constraints for design controls.
Resource settings like product, collection, page, article, blog, and image_picker connect theme UI to real Shopify data.
code:
[
  { "type": "text", "id": "heading", "label": "Heading" },
  { "type": "image_picker", "id": "image", "label": "Image" },
  { "type": "range", "id": "columns", "min": 2, "max": 6, "step": 1, "default": 4, "label": "Columns" },
  { "type": "select", "id": "alignment", "label": "Alignment", "options": [{"value":"left","label":"Left"},{"value":"center","label":"Center"}] },
  { "type": "color", "id": "accent", "label": "Accent color" }
]

quiz.question: Why should you prefer select/range over free text for design controls?
options: It makes the UI less safe
It constrains input and protects layout consistency
quiz.explanation: Constrained inputs reduce invalid configurations.

quiz.question: Which setting type is best for merchant-chosen products?
quiz.explanation: Resource picker settings should be used for product references.

videos.title: Introduction to settings_schema.json and settings_data.json
videos.description: Explains why theme settings are structured the way they are.

videos.title: How to Create Shopify Theme Settings Schema
videos.description: Practical setup of multiple setting types in schema.

title: settings_schema.json Deep Dive
summary: Understand global theme settings, presets, defaults, settings_data.json, and the mental model behind theme-wide configuration.
concepts: settings_schema.json defines top-level theme settings groups, not just per-section settings.
settings_data.json stores the merchant's chosen values for schema-defined options.
Global settings are useful for brand tokens, social links, typography, and cross-theme defaults.
A strong settings architecture prevents repetitive per-section configuration.

quiz.question: What stores the merchant's chosen values?
quiz.explanation: settings_data.json stores current selected values.

quiz.question: When are global theme settings useful?
options: For brand-wide defaults and reusable configuration
quiz.explanation: Global settings centralize reusable theme-wide options.

videos.title: Introduction to settings_schema.json and settings_data.json
videos.description: Focused explanation of settings_schema.json and settings_data.json.

videos.title: How to Create Shopify Theme Settings Schema
videos.description: Hands-on creation of theme settings and schema groups.

title: Dynamic Section Rendering
summary: Update sections asynchronously, combine JSON templates with partial refreshes, and build Dawn-style high-performance UX.
concepts: JSON templates define layout composition while Section Rendering API updates HTML fragments on demand.
You can request one or many sections and replace targeted DOM containers after an AJAX action.
This pattern is central to cart drawers, predictive updates, merchandising refreshes, and variant-driven UI.
Keep IDs and selectors predictable so replacement logic stays maintainable.

quiz.question: What does the Section Rendering API return?
quiz.explanation: It returns HTML snippets for the requested sections.

quiz.question: What is a common use case?
options: Refreshing a cart drawer without full reload
quiz.explanation: Partial storefront refreshes are a primary use case.

videos.title: Section Rendering API & Bundled Section Rendering
videos.description: Why and when to use Section Rendering API in modern themes.

videos.title: Shopify Section Rendering API – Update Sections Without Reload
videos.description: Practical partial-refresh example without page reload.

title: Creating Metafields (UI + Liquid)
summary: Create metafield definitions in admin, attach data to products and collections, and render them safely in Liquid.
concepts: Metafields store structured custom data on Shopify resources like products, variants, collections, pages, and customers.
Definitions make metafields typed, validated, and visible in the admin UI.
In Liquid, access metafields via namespace.key and use .value when the field type returns structured data.
Metafields are ideal when the data belongs to a specific resource instead of a reusable content model.

quiz.question: Where should product-specific custom data usually live?
quiz.explanation: Metafields are the right home for resource-specific custom data.

quiz.question: Why create a metafield definition first?
options: To get type safety and admin usability
quiz.explanation: Definitions give validation, type safety, and better editor UX.

videos.title: Shopify Metaobjects vs Metafields
videos.description: Clear explanation of metafields vs metaobjects and when to use each.

videos.title: Create custom data models with metafields and metaobjects
videos.description: Hands-on metafields and custom data modeling walkthrough.

title: Metafields via API, Types, and Validation
summary: Create metafield definitions through APIs, understand field types, and design validation rules that support content quality.
concepts: Admin API can create and update metafield definitions for app-managed workflows.
Field types include single_line_text_field, number_integer, rich_text_field, dimension, rating, file_reference, list types, and more.
Validation rules prevent bad content and reduce fragile Liquid conditions.
The best data model starts with content design, not with Liquid implementation alone.

quiz.question: Why add validation to a metafield definition?
options: To protect content quality and developer assumptions
quiz.explanation: Validation keeps custom data reliable.

quiz.question: Which API is commonly used for metafield definition creation now?
quiz.explanation: Definitions are handled through admin-side APIs.

videos.title: Shopify Metaobjects vs Metafields
videos.description: High-level framing of structured custom data types.

videos.title: Creating and Using Shopify Metaobjects
videos.description: Practical examples of building and using structured custom content.

title: Metaobjects and Structured Content Systems
summary: Create reusable content models such as FAQs, authors, badges, ingredients, or buying guides with metaobjects.
concepts: Metaobjects are reusable content entries with typed fields, ideal for structured content used across pages.
You can reference metaobjects from metafields or theme settings to create powerful content systems.
Metaobjects are better than hardcoded sections when content is shared across many surfaces.
They shine in editorial stores, brand-heavy merchandising, and large catalogs.

quiz.question: When is a metaobject better than a plain product metafield?
options: When the content is reusable and structured
When you only need one short note
When you need a CSS variable
quiz.explanation: Reusable structured content is a strong metaobject use case.

quiz.question: What is a common metaobject use case?
options: Authors or FAQs reused across pages
quiz.explanation: Metaobjects work well for shared structured entries.

videos.title: Shopify Metaobjects vs Metafields
videos.description: Conceptual framing for when to use metaobjects.

videos.title: Creating and Using Shopify Metaobjects
videos.description: Practical build examples that turn metaobjects into reusable theme content.

title: Public vs Custom vs Embedded Apps
summary: Understand distribution models, installation flow, merchant fit, and where embedded apps belong in the Shopify ecosystem.
concepts: Public apps are designed for broad merchant distribution while custom apps solve specific store needs.
Embedded apps live inside the Shopify admin and feel native with App Bridge and Polaris patterns.
The right app type depends on audience, maintenance model, support scope, and review requirements.
Architecture choices change when you target one merchant versus many merchants.
code:
App type decision guide:
- Public app: many merchants, app review, broad support
- Custom app: one merchant or tightly controlled use case
- Embedded app: admin-native UX using App Bridge + embedded surfaces

quiz.question: Which app type is usually best for a one-store internal workflow?
quiz.explanation: Custom apps are often the right fit for single-merchant/internal workflows.

quiz.question: What is a key benefit of embedded apps?
options: They feel native inside Shopify admin
quiz.explanation: Embedded apps create an admin-native merchant experience.

videos.title: How to create a Shopify App - An overview
videos.description: Strong overview of how Shopify app development works.

videos.title: How To Create a Shopify App using Shopify CLI
videos.description: Practical app creation flow using Shopify CLI.

title: App Bridge Basics
summary: Use App Bridge to build embedded admin experiences, navigation, actions, and contextual app behavior.
concepts: App Bridge helps embedded apps integrate with Shopify admin navigation, actions, and context.
It works alongside session tokens, authentication, and modern app frameworks.
App Bridge keeps embedded apps closer to native Shopify UX expectations.
Use it intentionally: merchant productivity matters more than flashy UI.

quiz.question: What is App Bridge mainly for?
options: Embedded app integration with Shopify admin
quiz.explanation: App Bridge is for embedded app UX integration.

quiz.question: Why does App Bridge matter?
options: It helps apps feel native inside Shopify admin
quiz.explanation: It aligns embedded app behavior with admin context.

videos.title: Getting Started with Shopify App Bridge
videos.description: Conceptual introduction to embedded app UX.

videos.title: Shopify Polaris and App Bridge with examples
videos.description: Practical App Bridge and Polaris examples.

title: Theme App Extensions
summary: Ship app blocks and app embeds without asking merchants to manually edit theme code.
concepts: Theme app extensions let apps add storefront functionality through app blocks and embeds.
This is safer and more maintainable than asking merchants to paste Liquid into theme files.
Extensions separate app-owned code from merchant-owned theme code.
Good extension UX respects merchant control, performance, and fallback states.

quiz.question: Why are theme app extensions preferred over manual theme edits?
options: They remove the need for storefront UI
They disable merchants from controlling placement
quiz.explanation: Extensions reduce brittle theme modifications and improve app isolation.

quiz.question: What can theme app extensions provide?
quiz.explanation: App blocks and embeds are the main storefront extension surfaces.

videos.title: How to create a Shopify App - An overview
videos.description: Broader app context before extension-specific implementation.

videos.title: Theme App Extension Tutorial: Build an Embedded App
videos.description: Theme app extension build walkthrough with an embedded app.

title: Introduction to Shopify Functions
summary: Learn what Shopify Functions are, where they run, and how they replace many old script-based customizations.
concepts: Shopify Functions extend backend commerce logic such as discounts, shipping, validation, and cart behavior.
They run in Shopify infrastructure, not as Liquid snippets or browser JavaScript.
Functions are a key part of modern extensibility and are important for advanced commerce workflows.
Use them when business rules belong near checkout or cart decision logic, not in theme presentation code.
code:
Common Function use cases:
- Product / order discounts
- Shipping customizations
- Cart and checkout validations
- Delivery or payment customizations

Think: backend commerce logic, not storefront rendering.

quiz.question: Where do Shopify Functions run?
quiz.explanation: Functions run server-side within Shopify extensibility surfaces.

quiz.question: What kind of problem suits Functions best?
options: Backend commerce rules like discounts and validations
quiz.explanation: Functions are for commerce logic, not storefront presentation.

videos.title: Shopify Functions - Overview and how to get started
videos.description: High-level mental model for Shopify Functions.

videos.title: Getting Started with Shopify Functions
videos.description: Step-by-step working setup for a real Function.

title: Admin API (REST + GraphQL)
summary: Understand the Admin API, compare REST and GraphQL, and choose the right approach for integrations and apps.
concepts: Admin API gives apps secure access to merchant data and operations.
GraphQL is now the strategic default for many new Shopify admin workflows because it is flexible and efficient.
REST still matters in legacy workflows and in existing integrations you may maintain.
Good API work starts with scopes, rate limits, and careful resource ownership.

quiz.question: What is a common reason teams prefer GraphQL?
options: It lets you request exactly the fields you need
quiz.explanation: GraphQL reduces over-fetching and supports flexible queries.

quiz.question: What should you check before making admin requests?
quiz.explanation: API scopes are critical to secure access.

videos.title: Shopify API for Beginners (2026) – Full Guide
videos.description: Beginner-friendly Shopify API overview.

videos.title: Shopify Admin API Tutorial | GraphQL & Rest Queries
videos.description: Practical REST and GraphQL request examples.

title: Storefront API
summary: Use the Storefront API for headless storefronts, custom frontends, and external experiences that need product and cart data.
concepts: Storefront API exposes product, collection, cart, checkout-related, and customer-facing commerce data.
It is designed for headless builds, custom buying experiences, and non-theme frontends.
The API uses GraphQL and public access tokens with carefully controlled access.
It is especially important in Hydrogen and custom storefront scenarios.

quiz.question: Which API is most relevant for headless storefront product data?
quiz.explanation: The Storefront API is designed for custom storefront experiences.

quiz.question: What style of API does the Storefront API use?
quiz.explanation: It is GraphQL-based.

videos.title: Add Shopify Products to Any Website (Buy Button and Storefront API)
videos.description: Strong intro to Storefront API use cases.

videos.title: How to create Shopify Storefront API access token (2026)
videos.description: Token setup and access workflow for Storefront API.

title: Webhooks Setup and HMAC Verification
summary: Subscribe to events, verify signatures, and process webhook payloads safely in real integrations.
concepts: Webhooks push event data to your app when store data changes, instead of forcing constant polling.
You must verify the HMAC signature using your app secret to trust the payload.
Process webhooks idempotently because deliveries can repeat.
Keep webhook handlers fast and move slow processing to queues or jobs when needed.

quiz.question: Why verify a webhook HMAC?
options: To confirm the payload really came from Shopify
quiz.explanation: HMAC validation protects you from spoofed requests.

quiz.question: Why should webhook handlers be idempotent?
options: Because webhooks can be retried or duplicated
quiz.explanation: Webhook delivery can repeat, so handlers must be safe to run more than once.

videos.title: Getting Started with Shopify webhooks using the Shopify Admin
videos.description: Explains setup, events, and delivery model.

videos.title: How to Verify Shopify Webhooks
videos.description: Step-by-step HMAC verification example.

title: Real-World API Workflows
summary: Combine Admin API, Storefront API, webhooks, queues, and background jobs into production-worthy data flows.
concepts: Real systems rarely use one API in isolation; they combine reads, writes, events, retries, and logs.
A common pattern is Admin API for setup, webhooks for changes, and background jobs for heavy processing.
Good workflow design includes observability, retries, idempotency, and rate-limit awareness.
Think in business workflows, not only in endpoint calls.
code:
Example workflow:
1. App installs and requests scopes
2. App reads products through Admin API
3. App subscribes to product/update webhook
4. Webhook queues re-sync job
5. Dashboard shows latest sync status and errors

quiz.question: What makes an API workflow production-ready?
options: Retries, logging, idempotency, and clear ownership
quiz.explanation: Production systems need reliability, not just a working demo.

quiz.question: Why combine webhooks with background jobs?
options: To keep handlers fast and process heavy work safely
quiz.explanation: Fast handlers plus queued processing is a robust pattern.

videos.title: Shopify API for Beginners (2026) – Full Guide
videos.description: Useful top-down API systems overview.

videos.title: How to Use the Shopify API and Make GraphQL Requests
videos.description: Practical API request workflow that maps well to real integrations.

title: Cart API (add / remove / update items)
summary: Use the Ajax Cart API to add items, change quantities, remove lines, and read cart state without full page reloads.
concepts: The Ajax API is central to theme-side cart interactions in non-headless storefronts.
Use /cart/add.js, /cart/change.js, /cart/update.js, and /cart.js for cart state operations.
Always show pending, success, and error states during cart actions.
Pair cart requests with partial UI refreshes for a polished experience.

quiz.question: Which endpoint is used to add a cart item?
quiz.explanation: Add items with /cart/add.js.

quiz.question: Why is loading state important for cart actions?
options: It communicates progress and prevents confused repeat clicks
quiz.explanation: Users need clear feedback during asynchronous cart updates.

videos.title: Shopify Liquid Tutorial - Shopify Ajax API
videos.description: Core theory of Shopify's theme-side Ajax APIs.

videos.title: Creating a Shopify ajax cart drawer
videos.description: Building a cart drawer with real requests and UI updates.

title: Section Rendering API
summary: Request fresh HTML for one or more sections and swap it into the page after cart or product interactions.
concepts: Section Rendering API is often the missing piece between Ajax data changes and full visual UI updates.
You can use it after cart operations, filtering, product option changes, or bundled cart updates.
It lets the server keep theme logic authoritative while the frontend stays fast.
This is often cleaner than rebuilding markup entirely in JavaScript.

quiz.question: Why use Section Rendering API instead of rebuilding all HTML manually?
quiz.explanation: It reuses server-rendered theme logic and keeps frontend code simpler.

quiz.question: What does the request return?
options: A JSON map of section IDs to HTML markup
quiz.explanation: The response maps requested section IDs to rendered HTML snippets.

videos.title: Section Rendering API & Bundled Section Rendering
videos.description: Conceptual intro to partial refreshes.

videos.title: Shopify Section Rendering API – Update Sections Without Reload
videos.description: Practical implementation of DOM section replacement.

title: Pub/Sub Event System
summary: Use a lightweight event system so product forms, drawers, badges, and recommendations stay decoupled.
concepts: A pub/sub pattern keeps theme JavaScript modular when many components react to the same event.
Cart updated, variant changed, and section refreshed are common event channels.
This helps large themes avoid tight coupling between unrelated DOM modules.
Good event names and payload contracts matter as much as the code itself.

quiz.question: Why use pub/sub in a theme?
options: To make modules more tightly coupled
To let separate components react to shared events cleanly
quiz.explanation: Pub/sub reduces direct dependencies between modules.

quiz.question: Which is a good pub/sub use case?
options: Cart updated event triggering multiple UI refreshes
quiz.explanation: Shared cart or variant events are ideal for pub/sub.

videos.title: Using Javascript and The Custom Elements Web API in Shopify
videos.description: Component-oriented JavaScript thinking inside Shopify themes.

videos.title: Breakdown the Dawn Cart using AJAX API + Section Rendering
videos.description: A Dawn cart breakdown showing real event-driven refresh behavior.

title: Dynamic Cart Updates without Reload
summary: Combine cart requests, optimistic UI, section rendering, and a small event system to build a polished cart drawer.
concepts: The best cart experiences update line items, totals, badges, and drawers in one coherent flow.
You need request orchestration, loading states, error handling, and partial refresh logic.
Keep your cart module focused on predictable lifecycle steps.
This pattern is a major theme-development skill for real storefront work.

quiz.question: What makes a dynamic cart feel polished?
options: Coordinated state, UI refresh, and feedback
quiz.explanation: Good cart UX combines backend updates with clear UI states.

quiz.question: What commonly updates after a line item change?
options: Drawer contents, icon bubble, totals, and notices
quiz.explanation: A complete cart experience updates multiple linked UI surfaces.

videos.title: Section Rendering API & Bundled Section Rendering
videos.description: Why partial rendering matters in live cart UX.

videos.title: Breakdown the Dawn Cart using AJAX API + Section Rendering

title: Shopify CLI Setup and Usage
summary: Install Shopify CLI, log in, run local development, pull theme code, and use core theme/app commands.
concepts: Shopify CLI is the standard local development tool for themes, apps, and extensions.
Use it to run theme dev servers, create apps, and push or pull theme code safely.
CLI-based workflow improves speed, previews, and source control hygiene.
A strong local setup reduces mistakes before deployment.
code:
shopify auth login
shopify theme dev
shopify theme pull
shopify theme push --unpublished
shopify app init

quiz.question: What is shopify theme dev primarily used for?
options: Running a local theme development workflow
quiz.explanation: It runs a live local theme development environment.

quiz.question: Why prefer CLI workflow over editing production themes directly?
options: It supports safer previews, version control, and testing
quiz.explanation: CLI improves safety and speed in development.

videos.title: Getting started with the Shopify CLI (theme development)
videos.description: CLI basics and what the tool is for.

videos.title: How to use Shopify CLI to develop faster
videos.description: Useful setup and speed-focused CLI workflow tips.

title: Working with the Dawn Theme
summary: Study Dawn as a reference architecture for modern Shopify theme patterns, including sections, JS, and product UX.
concepts: Dawn is Shopify's reference theme for modern Online Store 2.0 patterns.
Studying Dawn teaches section structure, performance habits, product UX conventions, and JS architecture.
You do not need to copy Dawn blindly; you need to understand its patterns.
Use Dawn as a benchmark for quality, accessibility, and maintainable storefront behavior.
code:
Dawn study checklist:
- main-product section
- cart drawer / cart notification patterns
- global.js and component events
- product media and variant changes
- section schema conventions
- localization patterns

quiz.question: Why study Dawn?
options: Because it is the only valid theme
Because it is a strong reference implementation for modern theme patterns
quiz.explanation: Dawn is valuable as a reference architecture.

quiz.question: What is the best way to use Dawn?
options: Study patterns, decisions, and implementation quality
quiz.explanation: Treat Dawn as a learning reference, not a blind copy source.

videos.title: Shopify's Dawn Theme - A Closer Look
videos.description: Strong overview of what Dawn teaches developers.

videos.title: Building on Dawn Theme
videos.description: Real coding series built on Dawn.

title: Introduction to Hydrogen
summary: Learn when headless makes sense, how Hydrogen uses React and the Storefront API, and how to think about Oxygen deployment.
concepts: Hydrogen is Shopify's React framework for headless storefronts.
It combines Storefront API access, commerce primitives, and opinionated app structure.
Headless is powerful but adds complexity, so use it for real business reasons rather than trend-following.
Hydrogen plus Oxygen is a complete path for Shopify-managed headless deployment.

quiz.question: When is Hydrogen a strong fit?
options: When a project truly needs headless flexibility and custom experience control
quiz.explanation: Headless should be chosen for clear business and technical reasons.

quiz.question: Which API powers Hydrogen storefront data?
quiz.explanation: Hydrogen relies heavily on the Storefront API.

videos.title: Shopify Hydrogen in 10 Minutes
videos.description: Quick mental model for Hydrogen and headless Shopify.

videos.title: Set Up a Headless Storefront with Hydrogen and Oxygen

title: Development Workflow Best Practices
summary: Use Git, environments, QA, deployment checklists, and handoff habits so your Shopify work ships safely and stays maintainable.
concepts: A professional workflow includes source control, local development, preview themes, staged releases, and rollback awareness.
QA should cover happy paths, empty states, accessibility, performance, and merchant settings.
Handoff quality matters: documentation, SOPs, known limits, and support expectations prevent chaos later.
This is the difference between a good demo and a professional delivery workflow.
code:
git checkout -b feature/cart-upsell
shopify theme dev
shopify theme push --unpublished
# QA preview theme
shopify theme push --theme 123456789
# Release after signoff

quiz.question: What makes a Shopify delivery workflow professional?
options: Version control, preview environments, QA, and clear handoff
quiz.explanation: Professional delivery is process + code quality.

quiz.question: Which item belongs in a strong client handoff?
options: Release notes, SOPs, and known limitations
quiz.explanation: Clarity and maintainability matter after launch too.

videos.title: Shopify Theme Development - Introduction, Setting Up Local Development
videos.description: Good setup and development habits from the start.

videos.title: Shopify Theme Developer Tutorial: Workflow and Deployment
videos.description: Deployment-oriented workflow and release process.

title: Polaris, GraphiQL, VS Code, and Partner Dashboard
summary: Round out your toolkit with Shopify's admin UI system, API explorers, local editor tooling, and partner operations.
concepts: Polaris provides design language and UI components for merchant-facing admin experiences.
GraphiQL and API explorers help you inspect schemas, test queries, and learn faster.
VS Code plus Shopify extensions and linting improve day-to-day developer speed.
Partner Dashboard is where you manage apps, stores, credentials, and distribution setup.

quiz.question: What is Polaris mainly for?
quiz.explanation: Polaris is Shopify's admin-oriented UI system.

quiz.question: What is GraphiQL useful for?
quiz.explanation: GraphiQL is ideal for schema exploration and query testing.

videos.title: Shopify GraphiQL Admin API Explained
videos.description: Clear GraphiQL walkthrough for Admin API exploration.

videos.title: How to Create a Shopify App in the Partner Dashboard
videos.description: Partner Dashboard setup and app creation workflow.

title: Checkout UI Extensions
summary: Extend checkout with UI components, validation, and workflow-aware messaging on extensible checkout surfaces.
concepts: Checkout UI Extensions let you add safe, structured UI to supported checkout targets.
They are part of modern checkout extensibility and matter especially for Shopify Plus projects.
Use them for messaging, upsells, validation support, and operational checkout workflows.
Always consider performance, responsiveness, and merchant configurability.

quiz.question: What do Checkout UI Extensions add?
options: Arbitrary theme code on product pages
Structured custom UI on supported checkout targets
quiz.explanation: They extend supported checkout surfaces with safe UI components.

quiz.question: Why are they important?
options: They power modern checkout extensibility workflows
quiz.explanation: Checkout extensibility is now a major advanced Shopify capability.

videos.title: Shopify Checkout UI Extensions
videos.description: Conceptual introduction to checkout extension surfaces.

videos.title: Building a Shopify Checkout UI Extension from Scratch
videos.description: Practical scratch build of a checkout UI extension.
